promptBlocks.text: Which goal best describes Logic, Arguments, and Meaning?

options.label: Review how premises support conclusions and how validity differs from truth, consistency, and contradiction.
Memorize notation without connecting it to reasoning.
Skip the authored sequence and guess from surface wording.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Review how premises support conclusions and how validity differs from truth, consistency, and contradiction.

promptBlocks.text: In “Every cached result is stale; therefore this response should be refreshed,” which claim is the conclusion?

options.label: This response should be refreshed.
Every cached result is stale.
The word “therefore.”

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “This response should be refreshed.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: An argument is a set of claims in which some claims—the premises—are presented as reasons for another claim—the conclusion. Finding the conclusion first often makes the structure easier to see. Common-mistake guidance: A topic is not automatically a conclusion. The conclusion is the claim the other statements are meant to support.

promptBlocks.text: Write one premise and one conclusion for an argument about whether a software change should be reviewed before release.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Example: “Unreviewed changes can hide defects” is a premise. “Therefore this change should be reviewed before release” is the conclusion.

promptBlocks.text: What does deductive validity guarantee?

options.label: Every premise is factually true.
If the premises are true, the conclusion cannot be false.
The conclusion is persuasive to every reader.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “If the premises are true, the conclusion cannot be false.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: Logic evaluates the relationship between premises and a conclusion. A valid pattern cannot have true premises and a false conclusion, even when the particular premises used in an example are not actually true. Common-mistake guidance: Do not reject a valid form merely because its subject matter is fictional or one premise is false.

promptBlocks.text: Explain why an argument may be valid even when one of its premises is false.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Validity concerns what would follow if the premises were true. Actual premise truth is a separate question used when judging soundness.

promptBlocks.text: Which description best fits two claims that cannot both be true?

options.label: They are inconsistent.
They are automatically valid.
They are equivalent.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “They are inconsistent.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: Validity describes an argument. Consistency describes whether claims could all be true together. Equivalence describes claims that always share a truth value. A contradiction cannot be true under any interpretation. Common-mistake guidance: Disagreement is not always contradiction. The claims must rule each other out under the same interpretation.

promptBlocks.text: Give a pair of contradictory claims and explain why no situation makes both true.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Example: “The door is open” and “The door is not open,” used of the same door at the same time and in the same sense, cannot both be true.

promptBlocks.text: In “Every cached result is stale; therefore this response should be refreshed,” which claim is the conclusion?

options.label: This response should be refreshed.
Every cached result is stale.
The word “therefore.”

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “This response should be refreshed.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: An argument is a set of claims in which some claims—the premises—are presented as reasons for another claim—the conclusion. Finding the conclusion first often makes the structure easier to see. Common-mistake guidance: A topic is not automatically a conclusion. The conclusion is the claim the other statements are meant to support.

promptBlocks.text: What does deductive validity guarantee?

options.label: Every premise is factually true.
If the premises are true, the conclusion cannot be false.
The conclusion is persuasive to every reader.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “If the premises are true, the conclusion cannot be false.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: Logic evaluates the relationship between premises and a conclusion. A valid pattern cannot have true premises and a false conclusion, even when the particular premises used in an example are not actually true. Common-mistake guidance: Do not reject a valid form merely because its subject matter is fictional or one premise is false.

promptBlocks.text: Analyze a short argument, identify its structure, and classify the logical relationship among its claims. Write one premise and one conclusion for an argument about whether a software change should be reviewed before release.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Example: “Unreviewed changes can hide defects” is a premise. “Therefore this change should be reviewed before release” is the conclusion.

promptBlocks.text: Analyze a short argument, identify its structure, and classify the logical relationship among its claims. Explain why an argument may be valid even when one of its premises is false.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Validity concerns what would follow if the premises were true. Actual premise truth is a separate question used when judging soundness.

promptBlocks.text: Analyze a short argument, identify its structure, and classify the logical relationship among its claims. Give a pair of contradictory claims and explain why no situation makes both true.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Example: “The door is open” and “The door is not open,” used of the same door at the same time and in the same sense, cannot both be true.

promptBlocks.text: A claim appears twice in an argument. How should a symbolization key represent it?

options.label: Use the same sentence letter both times.
Choose a new letter for every occurrence.
Replace it with a connective.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “Use the same sentence letter both times.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: Symbolization separates logical structure from subject matter. Start by assigning one sentence letter to each distinct atomic claim, then preserve repeated claims with the same letter. Common-mistake guidance: A sentence letter stands for a complete atomic claim, not for an individual word inside it.

promptBlocks.text: Give a symbolization key and form for: “If the build passes, deployment starts. The build passes. So deployment starts.”

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Let B mean “The build passes” and D mean “Deployment starts.” The form is B → D, B, therefore D.

promptBlocks.text: Which TFL form matches “The test passes if and only if the expected and actual values match”?

options.label: T ↔ M
T → M
T ∨ M

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “T ↔ M” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: TFL builds complex sentences with five connectives: ¬, ∧, ∨, →, and ↔. Translate the logical job of the English expression rather than matching words mechanically. Common-mistake guidance: “Only if” introduces a necessary condition and points toward the consequent of a conditional.

promptBlocks.text: Symbolize “The job runs unless the queue is paused,” using J and P.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: A standard TFL rendering is ¬P → J, equivalently J ∨ P in truth-functional contexts.

promptBlocks.text: Which expression is a well-formed TFL sentence?

options.label: A ∧ → B

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “(A ∧ ¬B)” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: A TFL sentence is built recursively: atomic letters are sentences; negating a sentence makes a sentence; and joining two sentences with a binary connective inside brackets makes a sentence. Common-mistake guidance: The visually central symbol is not necessarily the main connective; use the sentence’s construction and brackets.

promptBlocks.text: Identify the main connective and immediate components of ((A ∧ B) → ¬C).

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: The main connective is →. Its antecedent is (A ∧ B), and its consequent is ¬C.

promptBlocks.text: What should you do first when an English sentence supports two different TFL structures?

options.label: State the intended reading.
Choose the shorter formula automatically.
Remove every connective.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “State the intended reading.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: One English sentence can express different claims in different contexts. Formalization begins by stating the intended reading, especially when grouping changes the logical structure. Common-mistake guidance: Do not treat ordinary-language punctuation as a complete substitute for identifying the intended grouping.

promptBlocks.text: Give two bracketings for “A or B and C” and explain how their truth conditions differ.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: A ∨ (B ∧ C) needs A or both B and C. (A ∨ B) ∧ C always requires C and also at least one of A or B.

promptBlocks.text: Which sentence mentions an expression rather than using it?

options.label: “A ∧ B” contains a conjunction symbol.
A ∧ B is true on this valuation.
Ada designed an algorithm.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: ““A ∧ B” contains a conjunction symbol.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: Using a name talks about its bearer; mentioning a name talks about the expression. Quotation marks and metavariables keep the object language separate from the language used to describe it. Common-mistake guidance: Quotation marks change what the sentence is about; they are not decorative emphasis.

promptBlocks.text: Explain the difference between A and “A” in a sentence about TFL.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: A is an atomic sentence of the object language. “A” is a name for that written expression in the metalanguage.

promptBlocks.text: A claim appears twice in an argument. How should a symbolization key represent it?

options.label: Use the same sentence letter both times.
Choose a new letter for every occurrence.
Replace it with a connective.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “Use the same sentence letter both times.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: Symbolization separates logical structure from subject matter. Start by assigning one sentence letter to each distinct atomic claim, then preserve repeated claims with the same letter. Common-mistake guidance: A sentence letter stands for a complete atomic claim, not for an individual word inside it.

promptBlocks.text: Disambiguate a short passage, give its symbolization key and TFL form, and identify the main connective. Explain the difference between A and “A” in a sentence about TFL.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: A is an atomic sentence of the object language. “A” is a name for that written expression in the metalanguage.

promptBlocks.text: Which goal best describes Truth Tables?

options.label: Evaluate TFL formulas systematically and connect complete truth-table patterns to semantic classifications.
Memorize notation without connecting it to reasoning.
Skip the authored sequence and guess from surface wording.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Evaluate TFL formulas systematically and connect complete truth-table patterns to semantic classifications.

promptBlocks.text: When is a material conditional A → B false?

options.label: A is true and B is false.
A and B are both false.
A is false and B is true.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “A is true and B is false.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: A characteristic truth table defines a connective. Negation flips one value; conjunction requires both true; inclusive disjunction requires at least one true; a conditional is false only from true to false; a biconditional requires matching values. Common-mistake guidance: TFL disjunction is inclusive unless explicitly represented otherwise; both disjuncts may be true.

promptBlocks.text: Evaluate ¬A, A ∨ B, and A ↔ B when A is false and B is true.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: ¬A is true; A ∨ B is true; A ↔ B is false because the component values differ.

promptBlocks.text: Which expression is not truth-functional?

options.label: It is necessarily the case that P.
P and Q.
P if and only if Q.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “It is necessarily the case that P.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: A connective is truth-functional when component truth values uniquely determine the compound truth value. TFL deliberately models these connectives and leaves intensional notions such as necessity outside its scope. Common-mistake guidance: A connective appearing between two truth-valued claims is not automatically truth-functional.

promptBlocks.text: Explain why “because” cannot be represented merely by a TFL connective with a truth table.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Two component claims can keep the same truth values while their explanatory or causal relationship changes, so truth values alone do not determine “because.”

promptBlocks.text: How many rows does a complete truth table need for A, B, and C?

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “8” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: A complete table with n atomic letters has 2ⁿ rows. List valuations systematically, then calculate the smallest subsentences before the main connective. Common-mistake guidance: Do not calculate the main connective before the truth values of its immediate components are available.

promptBlocks.text: Describe the evaluation order for ¬(A ∨ B) ↔ C.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Evaluate A ∨ B, negate that result, then compare ¬(A ∨ B) with C using the biconditional.

promptBlocks.text: What would make a truth-table argument invalid?

options.label: A row with all premises true and the conclusion false.
A row with one premise false.
A row where every sentence is true.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “A row with all premises true and the conclusion false.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: Semantic tests ask what happens across valuations. A tautology is always true, a contradiction always false, equivalent formulas match on every row, and a valid argument has no row with true premises and a false conclusion. Common-mistake guidance: One matching row does not establish equivalence; the columns must match on all valuations.

promptBlocks.text: State the truth-table test for equivalence between formulas A and B.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Compare their columns on every valuation. They are equivalent exactly when their truth values match on every row.

promptBlocks.text: When is a material conditional A → B false?

options.label: A is true and B is false.
A and B are both false.
A is false and B is true.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “A is true and B is false.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: A characteristic truth table defines a connective. Negation flips one value; conjunction requires both true; inclusive disjunction requires at least one true; a conditional is false only from true to false; a biconditional requires matching values. Common-mistake guidance: TFL disjunction is inclusive unless explicitly represented otherwise; both disjuncts may be true.

promptBlocks.text: Which expression is not truth-functional?

options.label: It is necessarily the case that P.
P and Q.
P if and only if Q.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “It is necessarily the case that P.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: A connective is truth-functional when component truth values uniquely determine the compound truth value. TFL deliberately models these connectives and leaves intensional notions such as necessity outside its scope. Common-mistake guidance: A connective appearing between two truth-valued claims is not automatically truth-functional.

promptBlocks.text: Build or inspect a truth table, identify a decisive row, and justify a semantic verdict. State the truth-table test for equivalence between formulas A and B.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Compare their columns on every valuation. They are equivalent exactly when their truth values match on every row.

promptBlocks.text: Which goal best describes Semantic Reasoning in TFL?

options.label: Choose between complete and partial truth-table methods while respecting what TFL can and cannot represent.
Memorize notation without connecting it to reasoning.
Skip the authored sequence and guess from surface wording.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Choose between complete and partial truth-table methods while respecting what TFL can and cannot represent.

promptBlocks.text: Why can TFL fail to display the validity of “Ada is a dog, so Ada is an animal”?

options.label: TFL treats the internal predicate relationship as opaque.
TFL has no truth values.
The English argument has no conclusion.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “TFL treats the internal predicate relationship as opaque.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: TFL analyzes truth-functional structure, not internal subject–predicate structure, vagueness, modality, or explanation. A poor formalization can therefore miss genuine validity or introduce consequences the English claim did not have. Common-mistake guidance: A failed TFL validity test may reveal an inadequate formalization rather than bad reasoning in the original language.

promptBlocks.text: Name one feature of meaning that TFL deliberately leaves unanalyzed and explain the consequence.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Example: necessity is not truth-functional, so two true component claims do not determine whether either is necessarily true.

promptBlocks.text: Which value settles A ∧ B as false immediately?

options.label: Either conjunct is false.
Either conjunct is true.
The conjuncts have matching values.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “Either conjunct is false.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: A true disjunct settles a disjunction, a false conjunct settles a conjunction, and a conditional is settled true by a false antecedent or true consequent. For validity, focus on candidate rows where every premise is true. Common-mistake guidance: A shortcut is justified by a connective’s truth condition; it is not permission to omit a row that could still be decisive.

promptBlocks.text: Explain how to narrow a validity test whose first premise is A ∧ B.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Only rows with A true and B true can make that premise true, so all other rows can be ignored when searching for a counterexample.

promptBlocks.text: What is sufficient to show that an argument is invalid?

options.label: One valuation with true premises and a false conclusion.
One valuation with a false premise.
A complete table with no false cells.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “One valuation with true premises and a false conclusion.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: Universal claims need every valuation, but failures often need only one. To refute tautology, find one false row; to refute validity, find one row with true premises and a false conclusion. Common-mistake guidance: A row is a counterexample to validity only when every premise is true and the conclusion is false on that same row.

promptBlocks.text: Give a valuation showing that A ∨ B does not entail A.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Set A false and B true. Then A ∨ B is true while A is false, providing a countervaluation.

promptBlocks.text: Why can TFL fail to display the validity of “Ada is a dog, so Ada is an animal”?

options.label: TFL treats the internal predicate relationship as opaque.
TFL has no truth values.
The English argument has no conclusion.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “TFL treats the internal predicate relationship as opaque.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: TFL analyzes truth-functional structure, not internal subject–predicate structure, vagueness, modality, or explanation. A poor formalization can therefore miss genuine validity or introduce consequences the English claim did not have. Common-mistake guidance: A failed TFL validity test may reveal an inadequate formalization rather than bad reasoning in the original language.

promptBlocks.text: Which value settles A ∧ B as false immediately?

options.label: Either conjunct is false.
Either conjunct is true.
The conjuncts have matching values.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “Either conjunct is false.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: A true disjunct settles a disjunction, a false conjunct settles a conjunction, and a conditional is settled true by a false antecedent or true consequent. For validity, focus on candidate rows where every premise is true. Common-mistake guidance: A shortcut is justified by a connective’s truth condition; it is not permission to omit a row that could still be decisive.

promptBlocks.text: Find and explain a decisive valuation, or explain why TFL is too coarse to capture the original inference. Name one feature of meaning that TFL deliberately leaves unanalyzed and explain the consequence.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Example: necessity is not truth-functional, so two true component claims do not determine whether either is necessarily true.

promptBlocks.text: Find and explain a decisive valuation, or explain why TFL is too coarse to capture the original inference. Explain how to narrow a validity test whose first premise is A ∧ B.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Only rows with A true and B true can make that premise true, so all other rows can be ignored when searching for a counterexample.

promptBlocks.text: Find and explain a decisive valuation, or explain why TFL is too coarse to capture the original inference. Give a valuation showing that A ∨ B does not entail A.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Set A false and B true. Then A ∨ B is true while A is false, providing a countervaluation.

promptBlocks.text: Which goal best describes Natural Deduction Foundations?

options.label: Match proof goals and available formulas to introduction, elimination, and subproof strategies.
Memorize notation without connecting it to reasoning.
Skip the authored sequence and guess from surface wording.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Match proof goals and available formulas to introduction, elimination, and subproof strategies.

promptBlocks.text: What does a natural-deduction proof primarily display?

options.label: A sequence of rule-licensed inference steps.
Every possible valuation.
The historical origin of each premise.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “A sequence of rule-licensed inference steps.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: Truth tables test cases; natural deduction transforms formulas using rules. A derivation makes the route from assumptions to conclusion explicit and can scale without enumerating every valuation. Common-mistake guidance: A derivation is not a list of formulas that happen to be true; every non-assumption line needs a valid justification.

promptBlocks.text: Contrast the information supplied by a truth-table validity test with the information supplied by a derivation.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: A truth table shows there is no countervaluation. A derivation shows one structured route from the premises to the conclusion using accepted rules.

promptBlocks.text: Which rule directly derives A from A ∧ B?

options.label: Conjunction elimination
Conjunction introduction
Conditional introduction

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “Conjunction elimination” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: Introduction rules build a formula with a chosen main connective; elimination rules use such a formula. Subproofs temporarily add assumptions and discharge them only through the rule that licenses closure. Common-mistake guidance: A formula proved inside a subproof cannot be used outside after closure unless the governing rule permits it.

promptBlocks.text: Describe the subproof needed to derive A → B by conditional introduction.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Open a subproof assuming A, derive B within that subproof, then close it and infer A → B while discharging the assumption A.

promptBlocks.text: If the goal is A ∧ B, what backward plan is most direct?

options.label: Plan to derive A and B separately, then use ∧ introduction.
Assume ¬A and stop.
Apply ∧ elimination to the goal.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “Plan to derive A and B separately, then use ∧ introduction.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: The goal’s main connective suggests an introduction rule; the premises’ main connectives suggest elimination rules. When direct progress stalls, a carefully scoped subproof can expose the missing route. Common-mistake guidance: Writing the desired conclusion does not prove it; every planned line must be reachable by a rule from accessible earlier lines.

promptBlocks.text: Give a forward and backward planning observation for premises A → B and A with goal B ∧ A.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Forward: A → B and A yield B. Backward: the conjunction goal requires B and A; both are then available for ∧ introduction.

promptBlocks.text: What does a natural-deduction proof primarily display?

options.label: A sequence of rule-licensed inference steps.
Every possible valuation.
The historical origin of each premise.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “A sequence of rule-licensed inference steps.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: Truth tables test cases; natural deduction transforms formulas using rules. A derivation makes the route from assumptions to conclusion explicit and can scale without enumerating every valuation. Common-mistake guidance: A derivation is not a list of formulas that happen to be true; every non-assumption line needs a valid justification.

items.title: 5.0 Review: The Idea of Natural Deduction

promptBlocks.text: Which rule directly derives A from A ∧ B?

options.label: Conjunction elimination
Conjunction introduction
Conditional introduction

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “Conjunction elimination” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: Introduction rules build a formula with a chosen main connective; elimination rules use such a formula. Subproofs temporarily add assumptions and discharge them only through the rule that licenses closure. Common-mistake guidance: A formula proved inside a subproof cannot be used outside after closure unless the governing rule permits it.

promptBlocks.text: Plan and explain a short derivation, including every assumption, rule choice, and discharged subproof. Contrast the information supplied by a truth-table validity test with the information supplied by a derivation.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: A truth table shows there is no countervaluation. A derivation shows one structured route from the premises to the conclusion using accepted rules.

items.title: 5.0 Checkpoint: The Idea of Natural Deduction

promptBlocks.text: Plan and explain a short derivation, including every assumption, rule choice, and discharged subproof. Describe the subproof needed to derive A → B by conditional introduction.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Open a subproof assuming A, derive B within that subproof, then close it and infer A → B while discharging the assumption A.

promptBlocks.text: Plan and explain a short derivation, including every assumption, rule choice, and discharged subproof. Give a forward and backward planning observation for premises A → B and A with goal B ∧ A.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Forward: A → B and A yield B. Backward: the conjunction goal requires B and A; both are then available for ∧ introduction.

promptBlocks.text: Which goal best describes Proof Strategy?

options.label: Use additional and derived rules accurately while keeping proof-theoretic and semantic claims distinct.
Memorize notation without connecting it to reasoning.
Skip the authored sequence and guess from surface wording.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Use additional and derived rules accurately while keeping proof-theoretic and semantic claims distinct.

promptBlocks.text: Which pair licenses modus tollens?

options.label: A → B and ¬B
A → B and B
A ∨ B and ¬A

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “A → B and ¬B” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: Additional rules compress common derivations. Their convenience does not relax matching requirements: disjunctive syllogism needs a disjunction plus the negation of one disjunct, and modus tollens needs a conditional plus a negated consequent. Common-mistake guidance: Affirming the consequent—from A → B and B to A—is not modus tollens and is invalid.

promptBlocks.text: Use a named additional rule to derive ¬C from C → D and ¬D.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Modus tollens applies to C → D and ¬D, yielding ¬C.

promptBlocks.text: What does Γ ⊢ A assert?

options.label: A can be derived from assumptions in Γ.
A is false on one valuation.
Γ and A are strings with the same length.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “A can be derived from assumptions in Γ.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: Γ ⊢ A says there is a derivation of A from assumptions in Γ. Γ ⊨ A says no interpretation makes Γ true and A false. One is proof-theoretic; the other is semantic. Common-mistake guidance: The single and double turnstiles are not interchangeable notation merely because they often agree in TFL.

promptBlocks.text: Explain the difference between ⊢ A and ⊨ A without assuming soundness or completeness.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: ⊢ A reports the existence of a proof with no undischarged assumptions. ⊨ A reports truth on every valuation. Their equivalence is a further theorem, not part of either definition.

promptBlocks.text: What does deriving an additional rule establish?

options.label: Every use can be replaced by basic-rule steps.
The rule changes the truth table of a connective.
The rule permits inaccessible subproof lines.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “Every use can be replaced by basic-rule steps.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: A derived rule is admissible because every use can be replaced by a pattern of basic-rule steps. It shortens proofs without adding new derivable conclusions. Common-mistake guidance: A familiar valid argument form is not automatically a rule of the chosen proof system until its derivation or authorization is established.

promptBlocks.text: Explain why adding a derived rule can shorten proofs without increasing what the system can prove.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Because each derived-rule use expands into an existing basic derivation, it abbreviates proofs but reaches no conclusion unavailable before.

promptBlocks.text: Which claim expresses completeness?

options.label: If Γ ⊨ A, then Γ ⊢ A.
If Γ ⊢ A, then Γ ⊨ A.
If Γ ⊬ A, then Γ is empty.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “If Γ ⊨ A, then Γ ⊢ A.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: Soundness says derivable conclusions are semantically entailed: Γ ⊢ A implies Γ ⊨ A. Completeness says every semantic consequence is derivable: Γ ⊨ A implies Γ ⊢ A. Common-mistake guidance: Soundness and completeness are metatheoretic claims about the proof system, not labels for individual premises.

promptBlocks.text: State what soundness protects us from and what completeness promises.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Soundness prevents the rules from proving a semantic non-consequence. Completeness promises that the rules are strong enough to derive every TFL semantic consequence.

promptBlocks.text: Which pair licenses modus tollens?

options.label: A → B and ¬B
A → B and B
A ∨ B and ¬A

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “A → B and ¬B” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: Additional rules compress common derivations. Their convenience does not relax matching requirements: disjunctive syllogism needs a disjunction plus the negation of one disjunct, and modus tollens needs a conditional plus a negated consequent. Common-mistake guidance: Affirming the consequent—from A → B and B to A—is not modus tollens and is invalid.

promptBlocks.text: What does Γ ⊢ A assert?

options.label: A can be derived from assumptions in Γ.
A is false on one valuation.
Γ and A are strings with the same length.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “A can be derived from assumptions in Γ.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: Γ ⊢ A says there is a derivation of A from assumptions in Γ. Γ ⊨ A says no interpretation makes Γ true and A false. One is proof-theoretic; the other is semantic. Common-mistake guidance: The single and double turnstiles are not interchangeable notation merely because they often agree in TFL.

promptBlocks.text: Analyze a short proof strategy and explain how soundness, completeness, or derived-rule expansion justifies it. State what soundness protects us from and what completeness promises.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Soundness prevents the rules from proving a semantic non-consequence. Completeness promises that the rules are strong enough to derive every TFL semantic consequence.

promptBlocks.text: Which goal best describes First-Order Language?

options.label: Retrieve how domains, predicate places, quantifier scope, and identity determine a first-order symbolization.
Memorize notation without connecting it to reasoning.
Skip the authored sequence and guess from surface wording.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Retrieve how domains, predicate places, quantifier scope, and identity determine a first-order symbolization.

promptBlocks.text: With domain devices, d for the dashboard, and O for “is offline,” which formula says that some device is offline?

options.label: ∃xOx
Od
∀xOx

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “∃xOx” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: First-order logic opens atomic claims to show which objects are discussed and what is said of them. Names pick out domain members, predicates express properties or relations, variables hold places, and quantifiers say how widely those variables range. Common-mistake guidance: A quantified variable ranges over the entire stated domain, not merely the objects that happen to have names.

promptBlocks.text: Using domain servers, s for Sol, and R for “is restarting,” identify the job of each symbol in ∃xRx and Rs.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: The domain fixes what x may range over; x is a variable; ∃ is the existential quantifier; R is a one-place predicate; s is a name. ∃xRx says some server is restarting, while Rs says Sol is restarting.

promptBlocks.text: With domain services, A for “is an API,” and M for “is monitored,” which formula says every API is monitored?

options.label: ∀x(Ax → Mx)
∀x(Ax ∧ Mx)
∃x(Ax → Mx)

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “∀x(Ax → Mx)” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: Universal claims usually restrict a class with a conditional, while existential claims usually join the witness conditions with a conjunction. Paraphrasing first makes the intended quantifier, restriction, and scope visible. Common-mistake guidance: An existential conditional such as ∃x(Lx → Ex) is usually too weak because any non-log can make its conditional true.

promptBlocks.text: Using domain files, L for “is a log,” and E for “is encrypted,” symbolize “No log is encrypted” and give one equivalent formulation.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: One answer is ¬∃x(Lx ∧ Ex). An equivalent answer is ∀x(Lx → ¬Ex). Both exclude any file that is simultaneously a log and encrypted.

promptBlocks.text: If Rxy means “x reviews y,” which formula requires one shared item that everyone reviews?

options.label: ∃y∀xRxy
∀x∃yRxy
∀y∃xRxy

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “∃y∀xRxy” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: Many-place predicates preserve an ordered relation between objects. With multiple quantifiers, changing which quantifier has wider scope can change whether witnesses may differ or must be shared. Common-mistake guidance: Do not swap predicate places or quantifier order as a cosmetic rewrite; either change can alter the claim.

promptBlocks.text: Using domain people and Mxy for “x messages y,” compare ∀x∃yMxy with ∃y∀xMxy by describing a three-person case where only the first is true.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Let Ana message Bo, Bo message Cy, and Cy message Ana, with no other messages. Everyone messages someone, so ∀x∃yMxy is true. No single person is messaged by everyone, so ∃y∀xMxy is false.

promptBlocks.text: Which formula says that there are at least two alerts?

options.label: ∃x∃y(Ax ∧ Ay ∧ x ≠ y)
∃x∃y(Ax ∧ Ay)
∀x∀y(Ax → x = y)

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “∃x∃y(Ax ∧ Ay ∧ x ≠ y)” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: The identity sign says that two terms pick out one and the same object. Its negation guarantees distinct witnesses, which lets FOL express “someone else,” exclusions, and minimum or exact quantities. Common-mistake guidance: Two existential variables do not automatically denote two objects; add non-identity when distinctness matters.

promptBlocks.text: Using domain users, a for Ari, and Fxy for “x follows y,” symbolize “Ari follows everyone other than Ari.”

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: A direct rendering is ∀x(x ≠ a → Fax). This leaves open whether Ari follows Ari, because the conditional restricts the claim to users distinct from Ari.

promptBlocks.text: With domain devices, d for the dashboard, and O for “is offline,” which formula says that some device is offline?

options.label: ∃xOx
Od
∀xOx

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “∃xOx” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: First-order logic opens atomic claims to show which objects are discussed and what is said of them. Names pick out domain members, predicates express properties or relations, variables hold places, and quantifiers say how widely those variables range. Common-mistake guidance: A quantified variable ranges over the entire stated domain, not merely the objects that happen to have names.

promptBlocks.text: With domain services, A for “is an API,” and M for “is monitored,” which formula says every API is monitored?

options.label: ∀x(Ax → Mx)
∀x(Ax ∧ Mx)
∃x(Ax → Mx)

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “∀x(Ax → Mx)” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: Universal claims usually restrict a class with a conditional, while existential claims usually join the witness conditions with a conjunction. Paraphrasing first makes the intended quantifier, restriction, and scope visible. Common-mistake guidance: An existential conditional such as ∃x(Lx → Ex) is usually too weak because any non-log can make its conditional true.

promptBlocks.text: Build and explain a finite first-order symbolization key, then compare formulas that differ in quantifier order or identity. Using domain users, a for Ari, and Fxy for “x follows y,” symbolize “Ari follows everyone other than Ari.”

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: A direct rendering is ∀x(x ≠ a → Fax). This leaves open whether Ari follows Ari, because the conditional restricts the claim to users distinct from Ari.

promptBlocks.text: Which goal best describes Expressing First-Order Claims?

options.label: Review well-formed FOL sentences, Russell-style descriptions, and the scope choices that distinguish competing readings.
Memorize notation without connecting it to reasoning.
Skip the authored sequence and guess from surface wording.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Review well-formed FOL sentences, Russell-style descriptions, and the scope choices that distinguish competing readings.

promptBlocks.text: Which expression is an FOL sentence with no free variables?

options.label: ∀x∃yRxy
∀xRxy
Rxy

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “∀x∃yRxy” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: Names and variables are terms. Predicates applied to the right number of terms form atomic formulas, and connectives and quantifiers build larger formulas. A formula is a sentence only when every variable occurrence is bound. Common-mistake guidance: A quantifier binds only matching variable occurrences within its scope, not every variable visible nearby.

promptBlocks.text: For ∃y(Qy ∧ Rxy), list every bound and free variable occurrence and state whether the formula is a sentence.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Both occurrences of y are within ∃y and are bound. The occurrence of x is free, so the formula is not a sentence.

promptBlocks.text: Which formula says that exactly one deploy leader exists and that person is online?

options.label: ∃x(Dx ∧ ∀y(Dy → y = x) ∧ Ox)
∃x(Dx ∧ Ox)
∀x(Dx → Ox)

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “∃x(Dx ∧ ∀y(Dy → y = x) ∧ Ox)” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: A definite description aims at exactly one object. A Russell-style analysis replaces “the” with a quantified claim: at least one object is F, at most one object is F, and that object is G. Common-mistake guidance: ∃x(Cx ∧ Ax) supplies existence but not uniqueness, so it means only that some coordinator is available.

promptBlocks.text: Using C for “is a current coordinator” and A for “is available,” symbolize “The current coordinator is available” and label the existence, uniqueness, and availability parts.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: One solution is ∃x(Cx ∧ ∀y(Cy → y = x) ∧ Ax). Cx supplies a witness, ∀y(Cy → y = x) makes that witness unique, and Ax attributes availability. Equivalent bracketings that preserve those three conditions are acceptable.

promptBlocks.text: Which formula gives the shared-report reading of “Every analyst checked a report”?

options.label: ∃y(Ry ∧ ∀x(Ax → Cxy))
∀x(Ax → ∃y(Ry ∧ Cxy))
∀y(Ry → ∃x(Ax ∧ Cxy))

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “∃y(Ry ∧ ∀x(Ax → Cxy))” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: FOL makes the scope of every quantifier and connective explicit. An English sentence may leave open whether a witness is shared, whether negation is inside a quantifier, or which phrase “only” restricts. Common-mistake guidance: Do not grade an ambiguous English sentence against one formula until the intended scope reading has been stated.

promptBlocks.text: Give two readings of “Not every service passed a check” using Sx, Cy, and Pxy, and explain where negation or existential scope differs.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: A natural reading is ¬∀x(Sx → ∃y(Cy ∧ Pxy)), equivalently some service passed no check. A distinct reading can deny a shared-check claim: ¬∃y(Cy ∧ ∀x(Sx → Pxy)). The first denies coverage per service; the second only denies one common check.

promptBlocks.text: Which expression is an FOL sentence with no free variables?

options.label: ∀x∃yRxy
∀xRxy
Rxy

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “∀x∃yRxy” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: Names and variables are terms. Predicates applied to the right number of terms form atomic formulas, and connectives and quantifiers build larger formulas. A formula is a sentence only when every variable occurrence is bound. Common-mistake guidance: A quantifier binds only matching variable occurrences within its scope, not every variable visible nearby.

promptBlocks.text: Which formula says that exactly one deploy leader exists and that person is online?

options.label: ∃x(Dx ∧ ∀y(Dy → y = x) ∧ Ox)
∃x(Dx ∧ Ox)
∀x(Dx → Ox)

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “∃x(Dx ∧ ∀y(Dy → y = x) ∧ Ox)” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: A definite description aims at exactly one object. A Russell-style analysis replaces “the” with a quantified claim: at least one object is F, at most one object is F, and that object is G. Common-mistake guidance: ∃x(Cx ∧ Ax) supplies existence but not uniqueness, so it means only that some coordinator is available.

promptBlocks.text: Diagnose a first-order expression for free variables, uniqueness, and scope, then defend one explicit reading of an ambiguous claim. For ∃y(Qy ∧ Rxy), list every bound and free variable occurrence and state whether the formula is a sentence.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Both occurrences of y are within ∃y and are bound. The occurrence of x is free, so the formula is not a sentence.

promptBlocks.text: Diagnose a first-order expression for free variables, uniqueness, and scope, then defend one explicit reading of an ambiguous claim. Using C for “is a current coordinator” and A for “is available,” symbolize “The current coordinator is available” and label the existence, uniqueness, and availability parts.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: One solution is ∃x(Cx ∧ ∀y(Cy → y = x) ∧ Ax). Cx supplies a witness, ∀y(Cy → y = x) makes that witness unique, and Ax attributes availability. Equivalent bracketings that preserve those three conditions are acceptable.

promptBlocks.text: Diagnose a first-order expression for free variables, uniqueness, and scope, then defend one explicit reading of an ambiguous claim. Give two readings of “Not every service passed a check” using Sx, Cy, and Pxy, and explain where negation or existential scope differs.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: A natural reading is ¬∀x(Sx → ∃y(Cy ∧ Pxy)), equivalently some service passed no check. A distinct reading can deny a shared-check claim: ¬∃y(Cy ∧ ∀x(Sx → Pxy)). The first denies coverage per service; the second only denies one common check.

promptBlocks.text: Which goal best describes Interpretations?

options.label: Retrieve how domains, referents, extensions, satisfaction, and interpretation-wide definitions determine FOL semantics.
Memorize notation without connecting it to reasoning.
Skip the authored sequence and guess from surface wording.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Retrieve how domains, referents, extensions, satisfaction, and interpretation-wide definitions determine FOL semantics.

promptBlocks.text: In domain {α, β}, predicates P and Q have the same extension {α}, although their symbolization-key phrases have different meanings. What follows in FOL?

options.label: FOL cannot distinguish P from Q by meaning alone on this interpretation.
P and Q must have different extensions because their phrases mean different things.
Neither predicate can be true of α because two predicates mention it.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “FOL cannot distinguish P from Q by meaning alone on this interpretation.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: FOL is extensional: an interpretation fixes a nonempty domain, assigns names to domain members, and assigns each predicate an extension. If two predicates have the same extension, FOL cannot distinguish them merely because their ordinary-language meanings differ. One-place extensions contain objects; many-place extensions contain ordered tuples. Common-mistake guidance: Matching extensions do not make two ordinary-language predicates synonymous; they only make FOL unable to represent their difference in meaning on that interpretation.

promptBlocks.text: For domain {1, 2}, c denotes 1, d denotes 2, P and Q both have extension {2}, and R has extension {⟨1,2⟩}. Evaluate Pc, Qc, Pd, Qd, Rcd, and Rdc, then state what P and Q show about extensionality.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Pc and Qc are false; Pd and Qd are true because both predicates have extension {2}. Rcd is true and Rdc is false because ordered-pair direction matters. Even if P and Q were keyed to phrases with different meanings, FOL treats them alike here because their extensions match.

promptBlocks.text: Domain {a, b}; P has extension {a}. Which quantified sentence is true?

options.label: ∃xPx
∀xPx
¬∃xPx

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “∃xPx” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: Atomic truth follows from referents and extensions, and the familiar connectives retain their TFL truth conditions. A universal is true when every domain member satisfies its formula; an existential is true when at least one member does. Common-mistake guidance: Quantifiers range over all domain members, including unnamed members; checking only named objects is insufficient.

promptBlocks.text: In domain {1, 2, 3}, let e denote 2 and E have extension {2}. Evaluate ∀x(Ex → x = e), ∃xEx, and ∀xEx.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: ∀x(Ex → x = e) is true because the only E-object is the referent of e. ∃xEx is true with witness 2. ∀xEx is false because 1 and 3 are not in E.

promptBlocks.text: What establishes that Γ does not entail A in FOL?

options.label: An interpretation where every sentence in Γ is true and A is false.
An interpretation where A is true.
A derivation of A from Γ.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “An interpretation where every sentence in Γ is true and A is false.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: FOL semantic notions quantify over interpretations rather than TFL valuations. A validity is true in every interpretation; an entailment has no interpretation with true premises and false conclusion; satisfiable sentences share at least one model. Common-mistake guidance: One favorable interpretation proves satisfiability, not validity; validity requires truth in every interpretation.

promptBlocks.text: Classify the pair ∃xPx and ∀x¬Px as jointly satisfiable or unsatisfiable, and justify your answer semantically.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: They are jointly unsatisfiable. The existential requires a P-member, while the universal says every domain member is non-P, so no interpretation can make both true.

promptBlocks.text: In domain {α, β}, predicates P and Q have the same extension {α}, although their symbolization-key phrases have different meanings. What follows in FOL?

options.label: FOL cannot distinguish P from Q by meaning alone on this interpretation.
P and Q must have different extensions because their phrases mean different things.
Neither predicate can be true of α because two predicates mention it.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “FOL cannot distinguish P from Q by meaning alone on this interpretation.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: FOL is extensional: an interpretation fixes a nonempty domain, assigns names to domain members, and assigns each predicate an extension. If two predicates have the same extension, FOL cannot distinguish them merely because their ordinary-language meanings differ. One-place extensions contain objects; many-place extensions contain ordered tuples. Common-mistake guidance: Matching extensions do not make two ordinary-language predicates synonymous; they only make FOL unable to represent their difference in meaning on that interpretation.

promptBlocks.text: Domain {a, b}; P has extension {a}. Which quantified sentence is true?

options.label: ∃xPx
∀xPx
¬∃xPx

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “∃xPx” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: Atomic truth follows from referents and extensions, and the familiar connectives retain their TFL truth conditions. A universal is true when every domain member satisfies its formula; an existential is true when at least one member does. Common-mistake guidance: Quantifiers range over all domain members, including unnamed members; checking only named objects is insufficient.

promptBlocks.text: Evaluate a finite interpretation and use it to classify a semantic claim with an explicit witness or counterexample. For domain {1, 2}, c denotes 1, d denotes 2, P and Q both have extension {2}, and R has extension {⟨1,2⟩}. Evaluate Pc, Qc, Pd, Qd, Rcd, and Rdc, then state what P and Q show about extensionality.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Pc and Qc are false; Pd and Qd are true because both predicates have extension {2}. Rcd is true and Rdc is false because ordered-pair direction matters. Even if P and Q were keyed to phrases with different meanings, FOL treats them alike here because their extensions match.

promptBlocks.text: Evaluate a finite interpretation and use it to classify a semantic claim with an explicit witness or counterexample. In domain {1, 2, 3}, let e denote 2 and E have extension {2}. Evaluate ∀x(Ex → x = e), ∃xEx, and ∀xEx.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: ∀x(Ex → x = e) is true because the only E-object is the referent of e. ∃xEx is true with witness 2. ∀xEx is false because 1 and 3 are not in E.

promptBlocks.text: Evaluate a finite interpretation and use it to classify a semantic claim with an explicit witness or counterexample. Classify the pair ∃xPx and ∀x¬Px as jointly satisfiable or unsatisfiable, and justify your answer semantically.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: They are jointly unsatisfiable. The existential requires a P-member, while the universal says every domain member is non-P, so no interpretation can make both true.

promptBlocks.text: Which goal best describes Relational Reasoning?

options.label: Retrieve how countermodels, all-interpretation arguments, and named relation properties support semantic reasoning.
Memorize notation without connecting it to reasoning.
Skip the authored sequence and guess from surface wording.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Retrieve how countermodels, all-interpretation arguments, and named relation properties support semantic reasoning.

promptBlocks.text: Which interpretation refutes Pa ⊨ ∀xPx?

options.label: Domain {a, b}; a denotes a; P = {a}.
Domain {a}; a denotes a; P = {a}.
Domain {a, b}; a denotes a; P = {a, b}.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “Domain {a, b}; a denotes a; P = {a}.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: A single carefully chosen interpretation can refute a universal semantic claim. Work backward from the truth values required, choose the smallest useful domain, then assign predicate extensions and name referents to realize them. Common-mistake guidance: A counter-interpretation must satisfy every premise and falsify the conclusion at the same time.

promptBlocks.text: Give a two-object counter-interpretation to ∃xPx ⊨ ∀xPx, including the domain and extension of P.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Let the domain be {1, 2} and P = {1}. Then ∃xPx is true with witness 1, while ∀xPx is false because 2 is not P. Any isomorphic two-object model works.

promptBlocks.text: What is enough to prove that a set of FOL sentences is satisfiable?

options.label: One interpretation making all of them true.
One interpretation making one of them false.
Ten interpretations making all of them true.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “One interpretation making all of them true.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: One model proves satisfiability, and one countermodel disproves validity or entailment. Positive universal claims—validity, contradiction, equivalence, unsatisfiability, and entailment—require an argument covering every interpretation. Common-mistake guidance: Many supporting examples do not establish a claim quantified over every interpretation.

promptBlocks.text: Explain why checking 100 interpretations cannot prove an FOL sentence valid, while one false interpretation can prove it invalid.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Validity requires truth in every interpretation, and there are infinitely many interpretations, so 100 positive cases leave others open. A single false case directly contradicts the universal requirement and is decisive.

promptBlocks.text: Which formula expresses symmetry of R?

options.label: ∀x∀y(Rxy → Ryx)
∀xRxx
∀x∀y∀z((Rxy ∧ Ryz) → Rxz)

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “∀x∀y(Rxy → Ryx)” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: Relation properties add structure not guaranteed by an arbitrary two-place predicate. Reflexivity requires Rxx for every x; symmetry reverses every related pair; transitivity closes two-step paths; antisymmetry forces mutually related objects to be identical. Common-mistake guidance: Antisymmetric does not mean “never symmetric”; it forbids two distinct objects from being related both ways.

promptBlocks.text: For domain {1, 2} and R = {⟨1,1⟩, ⟨2,2⟩, ⟨1,2⟩}, classify R as reflexive, symmetric, transitive, and antisymmetric.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: R is reflexive because both self-pairs occur; not symmetric because ⟨1,2⟩ occurs without ⟨2,1⟩; transitive because every available two-step chain has its required result; and antisymmetric because no two distinct objects are related in both directions.

promptBlocks.text: Which interpretation refutes Pa ⊨ ∀xPx?

options.label: Domain {a, b}; a denotes a; P = {a}.
Domain {a}; a denotes a; P = {a}.
Domain {a, b}; a denotes a; P = {a, b}.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “Domain {a, b}; a denotes a; P = {a}.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: A single carefully chosen interpretation can refute a universal semantic claim. Work backward from the truth values required, choose the smallest useful domain, then assign predicate extensions and name referents to realize them. Common-mistake guidance: A counter-interpretation must satisfy every premise and falsify the conclusion at the same time.

promptBlocks.text: What is enough to prove that a set of FOL sentences is satisfiable?

options.label: One interpretation making all of them true.
One interpretation making one of them false.
Ten interpretations making all of them true.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “One interpretation making all of them true.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: One model proves satisfiability, and one countermodel disproves validity or entailment. Positive universal claims—validity, contradiction, equivalence, unsatisfiability, and entailment—require an argument covering every interpretation. Common-mistake guidance: Many supporting examples do not establish a claim quantified over every interpretation.

promptBlocks.text: Construct or diagnose a finite interpretation, then state exactly which semantic claim and relation properties it establishes. Give a two-object counter-interpretation to ∃xPx ⊨ ∀xPx, including the domain and extension of P.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Let the domain be {1, 2} and P = {1}. Then ∃xPx is true with witness 1, while ∀xPx is false because 2 is not P. Any isomorphic two-object model works.

promptBlocks.text: Construct or diagnose a finite interpretation, then state exactly which semantic claim and relation properties it establishes. Explain why checking 100 interpretations cannot prove an FOL sentence valid, while one false interpretation can prove it invalid.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Validity requires truth in every interpretation, and there are infinitely many interpretations, so 100 positive cases leave others open. A single false case directly contradicts the universal requirement and is decisive.

promptBlocks.text: Construct or diagnose a finite interpretation, then state exactly which semantic claim and relation properties it establishes. For domain {1, 2} and R = {⟨1,1⟩, ⟨2,2⟩, ⟨1,2⟩}, classify R as reflexive, symmetric, transitive, and antisymmetric.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: R is reflexive because both self-pairs occur; not symmetric because ⟨1,2⟩ occurs without ⟨2,1⟩; transitive because every available two-step chain has its required result; and antisymmetric because no two distinct objects are related in both directions.

promptBlocks.text: Which goal best describes Quantifier and Identity Proofs?

options.label: Retrieve quantifier-rule restrictions, proof-order strategy, conversion laws, and identity substitution without treating names as arbitrary by default.
Memorize notation without connecting it to reasoning.
Skip the authored sequence and guess from surface wording.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Retrieve quantifier-rule restrictions, proof-order strategy, conversion laws, and identity substitution without treating names as arbitrary by default.

promptBlocks.text: Which quantifier-rule use is licensed?

options.label: From ∀xPx infer Pa by ∀E.
From Pa, where a occurs in a premise, infer ∀xPx by ∀I.
From ∃xPx infer Pa directly, without a fresh-name subproof.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “From ∀xPx infer Pa by ∀E.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: The quantifier rules control when a quantified claim may yield an instance and when an instance may support a quantified claim. Universal elimination instantiates any name; existential introduction generalizes from a named case. Universal introduction requires a genuinely arbitrary name, and existential elimination requires a fresh temporary witness whose identity does not leak into the conclusion. Common-mistake guidance: A name used for ∀I must be arbitrary, and a name introduced inside ∃E must be fresh; a convenient letter is not automatically eligible.

promptBlocks.text: Give a line-by-line derivation of ∃xQx from ∀x(Px → Qx) and Pa, naming every quantifier and connective rule.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Reviewed derivation: 1. ∀x(Px → Qx) Premise. 2. Pa Premise. 3. Pa → Qa ∀E 1. 4. Qa →E 2,3. 5. ∃xQx ∃I 4. Self-check rubric: the instance preserves the same name throughout; →E cites both required lines; ∃I replaces the relevant name occurrence; every line is in dependency order. A different derivation is acceptable if each step is licensed, its citations are explicit, and no quantifier restriction is violated.

promptBlocks.text: To prove ∀x(Px → Qx) from ∀xPx and ∀xQx, which ordered plan is legitimate?

options.label: Choose a fresh name a; derive Pa and Qa by ∀E; assume Pa; reiterate Qa; close with →I; conclude ∀x(Px → Qx) by ∀I.
Conclude ∀x(Px → Qx) first, then choose a name that appears in a premise.
Use ∃E on ∀xPx and skip the conditional subproof.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “Choose a fresh name a; derive Pa and Qa by ∀E; assume Pa; reiterate Qa; close with →I; conclude ∀x(Px → Qx) by ∀I.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: Proof construction combines the familiar connective strategies with quantifier-specific moves. Work backward from a universal goal using a fresh arbitrary name, forward from an existential premise using a fresh-witness subproof, and delay existential introduction or universal elimination until the needed instance is clear. Common-mistake guidance: Do not generalize from a name that occurs in a premise or undischarged assumption; that object is no longer arbitrary.

promptBlocks.text: Construct a proof of ∀xQx from ∀x(Px → Qx) and ∀xPx, showing the exact order of instantiation, connective elimination, and generalization.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Reviewed derivation: 1. ∀x(Px → Qx) Premise. 2. ∀xPx Premise. 3. Pa → Qa ∀E 1, with fresh a. 4. Pa ∀E 2. 5. Qa →E 3,4. 6. ∀xQx ∀I 5. Self-check rubric: a is absent from all premises; both universal instances use the same arbitrary name; the conditional is eliminated before generalization; ∀I is the final step. A different derivation is acceptable if its dependency order is valid and the ∀I name remains arbitrary.

promptBlocks.text: Which is equivalent to ¬∃xPx?

options.label: ∀x¬Px
∃x¬Px
¬∀x¬Px

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “∀x¬Px” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: Quantifier conversion pairs negation with a quantifier switch: denying that everything is F is equivalent to saying something is not F, while denying that anything is F is equivalent to saying everything is not F. The conversion changes both the quantifier and the scope of negation. Common-mistake guidance: Pushing negation across a quantifier without switching ∀ and ∃ produces a non-equivalent formula.

promptBlocks.text: Derive or justify the equivalence between ¬∀x(Px → Qx) and ∃x(Px ∧ ¬Qx), showing each conversion or TFL equivalence used.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Reviewed chain: ¬∀x(Px → Qx) is equivalent by quantifier conversion to ∃x¬(Px → Qx); replace the conditional inside the quantifier with ¬Px ∨ Qx, then apply negation and De Morgan/double negation to obtain ∃x(Px ∧ ¬Qx). Self-check rubric: the outer ∀ becomes ∃; negation remains over the whole matrix until the TFL transformation; the final witness satisfies P and fails Q. A different derivation is acceptable if every equivalence preserves scope and the endpoints are interderivable.

promptBlocks.text: From a = b and Pa, which conclusion follows directly by =E?

options.label: Pb
a = a only
∀xPx

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “Pb” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: Identity introduction licenses a = a without premises. Identity elimination uses a = b plus a formula containing one name to replace one or more eligible occurrences with the other. Substitution transfers a property or relation; it does not manufacture identity from merely similar descriptions. Common-mistake guidance: Identity elimination needs an identity premise; sharing every listed predicate does not by itself prove that two names denote one object.

promptBlocks.text: Prove ∀x∀y(x = y → y = x) using =I, =E, conditional introduction, and universal introduction.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Reviewed derivation: choose fresh a and b; assume a = b; write a = a by =I; use =E with a = b to replace the first a in a = a, yielding b = a; discharge the assumption to get a = b → b = a; generalize over b and then a by ∀I. Self-check rubric: both names are fresh; =I creates only reflexive identity; =E performs the symmetry-producing substitution; the assumption is discharged before either ∀I. A different derivation is acceptable if it proves symmetry without assuming it and respects both universal-introduction restrictions.

promptBlocks.text: Which quantifier-rule use is licensed?

options.label: From ∀xPx infer Pa by ∀E.
From Pa, where a occurs in a premise, infer ∀xPx by ∀I.
From ∃xPx infer Pa directly, without a fresh-name subproof.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “From ∀xPx infer Pa by ∀E.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: The quantifier rules control when a quantified claim may yield an instance and when an instance may support a quantified claim. Universal elimination instantiates any name; existential introduction generalizes from a named case. Universal introduction requires a genuinely arbitrary name, and existential elimination requires a fresh temporary witness whose identity does not leak into the conclusion. Common-mistake guidance: A name used for ∀I must be arbitrary, and a name introduced inside ∃E must be fresh; a convenient letter is not automatically eligible.

promptBlocks.text: To prove ∀x(Px → Qx) from ∀xPx and ∀xQx, which ordered plan is legitimate?

options.label: Choose a fresh name a; derive Pa and Qa by ∀E; assume Pa; reiterate Qa; close with →I; conclude ∀x(Px → Qx) by ∀I.
Conclude ∀x(Px → Qx) first, then choose a name that appears in a premise.
Use ∃E on ∀xPx and skip the conditional subproof.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “Choose a fresh name a; derive Pa and Qa by ∀E; assume Pa; reiterate Qa; close with →I; conclude ∀x(Px → Qx) by ∀I.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: Proof construction combines the familiar connective strategies with quantifier-specific moves. Work backward from a universal goal using a fresh arbitrary name, forward from an existential premise using a fresh-witness subproof, and delay existential introduction or universal elimination until the needed instance is clear. Common-mistake guidance: Do not generalize from a name that occurs in a premise or undischarged assumption; that object is no longer arbitrary.

promptBlocks.text: Construct or diagnose a quantified proof, citing each line and checking fresh-name, scope, subproof, and identity-substitution conditions. Prove ∀x∀y(x = y → y = x) using =I, =E, conditional introduction, and universal introduction.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Reviewed derivation: choose fresh a and b; assume a = b; write a = a by =I; use =E with a = b to replace the first a in a = a, yielding b = a; discharge the assumption to get a = b → b = a; generalize over b and then a by ∀I. Self-check rubric: both names are fresh; =I creates only reflexive identity; =E performs the symmetry-producing substitution; the assumption is discharged before either ∀I. A different derivation is acceptable if it proves symmetry without assuming it and respects both universal-introduction restrictions.

promptBlocks.text: What proves that a CQ rule is derived rather than new?

options.label: A basic-rule derivation can replace every CQ use.
CQ changes the semantics of ∀ and ∃.
CQ may cite lines inside closed subproofs.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “A basic-rule derivation can replace every CQ use.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: A quantifier-conversion rule is derived when every use can be expanded into the basic quantifier and connective rules. The shortcut is safe because it compresses an available derivation rather than adding a new consequence. Common-mistake guidance: Calling a transformation intuitive does not establish it as a derived rule; the expansion must obey every basic-rule restriction.

promptBlocks.text: Outline a basic-rule derivation that justifies one direction of ¬∃xPx therefore ∀x¬Px, naming the contradiction and discharge steps.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Reviewed outline: choose fresh a; assume Pa; infer ∃xPx by ∃I; contradict ¬∃xPx; discharge Pa to obtain ¬Pa; because a is arbitrary and absent from the premise, infer ∀x¬Px by ∀I. Self-check rubric: the witness name is fresh; ∃I supplies the sentence contradicted by the premise; the assumption is discharged before ∀I; the final generalization meets its restriction. A different derivation is acceptable if it expands the CQ direction using only authorized basic rules.

promptBlocks.text: What is the most direct certificate that Γ does not entail A?

options.label: One interpretation making every sentence in Γ true and A false.
One proof of A from Γ.
A list of formulas with no truth assignments.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “One interpretation making every sentence in Γ true and A false.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: Derivability and semantic entailment are different definitions even when soundness and completeness connect them. A proof is a finite certificate for derivability. One interpretation refutes entailment, and a counter-interpretation may have any nonempty domain allowed by FOL. By contrast, establishing entailment semantically must cover every interpretation. Pick the method whose polarity matches the task. Common-mistake guidance: A model can prove satisfiability or refute entailment, but one favorable model cannot establish a claim about every interpretation.

promptBlocks.text: For ∀x(Px → Qx), ∃xPx ⊨ ∃xQx, give either a formal derivation or a semantic argument and explain why your certificate establishes entailment.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Reviewed derivation: take the existential premise with fresh witness a in an ∃E subproof; instantiate the universal as Pa → Qa; derive Qa; introduce ∃xQx; close ∃E. Self-check rubric: the witness is fresh; the conclusion contains no leaked witness name; the universal premise is instantiated inside the subproof; the final existential claim follows outside it. A different derivation is acceptable if it is formally licensed, or a semantic argument is acceptable if it covers every interpretation rather than one example.

promptBlocks.text: Which formula is in DNF?

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “(A ∧ ¬B) ∨ (¬A ∧ B)” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: DNF is a disjunction of conjunctions of literals; CNF is a conjunction of disjunctions of literals. Negation has minimal scope in both. A complete truth table constructs DNF from its true rows and CNF from its false rows, guaranteeing an equivalent formula. Common-mistake guidance: Normal form is a structural condition, not merely a formula that happens to use conjunction or disjunction.

promptBlocks.text: Construct one DNF and one CNF equivalent to A → B, and verify each against the conditional false row.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Reviewed answers include DNF ¬A ∨ B and CNF ¬A ∨ B. ¬A ∨ B contains two literals: it is DNF as a disjunction of one-literal conjunctions and CNF as one disjunctive clause. It is false only when A is true and B is false. Self-check rubric: only ¬, ∧, and ∨ appear; every negation has atomic scope; the DNF has no disjunction inside a conjunction; the CNF has no conjunction inside a disjunction; the truth values match A → B. A different derivation is acceptable if the proposed forms satisfy the structural tests and match all four rows.

promptBlocks.text: Why is disjunction alone not functionally complete?

options.label: Any formula built only from ∨ is true when every input is true, so it cannot express negation.
Disjunction has two inputs rather than one.
Disjunction cannot appear in a truth table.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “Any formula built only from ∨ is true when every input is true, so it cannot express negation.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: A set of connectives is functionally complete when formulas using only that set can realize every possible truth table. Normal forms use ¬, ∧, and ∨. After recovering ¬ and ∧ from NAND, define disjunction by A ∨ B ≡ ¬(¬A ∧ ¬B); the recovered {¬, ∧, ∨} normal-form basis is functionally complete. NOR supports the analogous recovery, so either NAND or NOR can serve alone. Common-mistake guidance: Showing that a basis expresses several familiar connectives is not enough unless those connectives are themselves known to generate every truth function.

promptBlocks.text: Using NAND notation A | B, express ¬A and A ∧ B, then explain why those definitions support functional completeness.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Reviewed definitions: ¬A is A | A; A ∧ B is (A | B) | (A | B). NAND first negates the conjunction, and the repeated NAND negates that result. Next recover disjunction with A ∨ B ≡ ¬(¬A ∧ ¬B), replacing ¬ and ∧ by those NAND definitions. Thus the recovered {¬, ∧, ∨} normal-form basis is functionally complete. Self-check rubric: evaluate the negation and conjunction definitions on every input row; show the disjunction bridge; use only NAND in the expanded formulas; connect the recovered basis to the normal-form theorem. A different derivation is acceptable if its NAND-only formulas have the required truth tables and the completeness argument includes the bridge to a known complete basis.

promptBlocks.text: What is the legitimate next step after ¬(¬A ∨ B)?

options.label: ¬¬A ∧ ¬B by De Morgan.
¬A ∧ B by commutativity.
A ∨ ¬B by identity elimination.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “¬¬A ∧ ¬B by De Morgan.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: Equivalent formulas may replace one another inside a larger formula without changing its truth value. A chain records one local law per step—such as conditional elimination, De Morgan, double negation, distribution, or absorption—so the whole transformation remains auditable. Common-mistake guidance: An algebraic-looking rewrite is not a proof of equivalence unless the replaced subformula and governing equivalence are identifiable.

promptBlocks.text: Give a named equivalence chain converting ¬(A ∧ (B ∨ C)) into DNF, one local replacement per line.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Reviewed chain: ¬(A ∧ (B ∨ C)); ¬A ∨ ¬(B ∨ C) by De Morgan; ¬A ∨ (¬B ∧ ¬C) by De Morgan. This is DNF: a disjunction whose second disjunct is a conjunction of literals. Self-check rubric: each line changes one displayed subformula under a named equivalence; negations finish with atomic scope; the last formula satisfies the DNF structural conditions; endpoints agree on every valuation. A different derivation is acceptable if every replacement is equivalence-preserving and the final formula is genuinely in DNF.

promptBlocks.text: What proves that a CQ rule is derived rather than new?

options.label: A basic-rule derivation can replace every CQ use.
CQ changes the semantics of ∀ and ∃.
CQ may cite lines inside closed subproofs.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Correct answer: “A basic-rule derivation can replace every CQ use.” because it matches the lesson's governing explanation: A quantifier-conversion rule is derived when every use can be expanded into the basic quantifier and connective rules. The shortcut is safe because it compresses an available derivation rather than adding a new consequence. Common-mistake guidance: Calling a transformation intuitive does not establish it as a derived rule; the expansion must obey every basic-rule restriction.

promptBlocks.text: Choose and complete a proof, countermodel, normal-form construction, or equivalence chain, then audit every dependency and transformation. Give a named equivalence chain converting ¬(A ∧ (B ∨ C)) into DNF, one local replacement per line.

reviewedSolutionBlocks.text: Reviewed chain: ¬(A ∧ (B ∨ C)); ¬A ∨ ¬(B ∨ C) by De Morgan; ¬A ∨ (¬B ∧ ¬C) by De Morgan. This is DNF: a disjunction whose second disjunct is a conjunction of literals. Self-check rubric: each line changes one displayed subformula under a named equivalence; negations finish with atomic scope; the last formula satisfies the DNF structural conditions; endpoints agree on every valuation. A different derivation is acceptable if every replacement is equivalence-preserving and the final formula is genuinely in DNF.
